description: Major transport infrastructure in Australia. Appraised using ATAP (Australian Transport Assessment and Planning) guidelines.

motorway_per_km.note: Wide range. Urban motorway tunnels at the upper end (e.g., WestConnex ~AUD 500m/km). Rural highway upgrades at the lower end.

rail_per_km.note: New metro lines at upper end. Reference: Melbourne Metro Tunnel ~AUD 300m/km.

typical_benefits.monetisation: ATAP PV2: work ~AUD 50/hr, commuting ~AUD 18/hr, leisure ~AUD 15/hr (indicative, check latest ATAP databook)
ATAP: statistical life value ~AUD 5.9m (2025). Serious injury ~AUD 680k.
Present separately. Infrastructure Australia assessment criteria include WEBs.

typical_benefits.description: For level crossing removals: reduced severance, improved walkability, new open space
typical_benefits.monetisation: Hedonic pricing or stated preference. Difficult to monetise robustly.

default_parameters.appraisal_period_note: ATAP recommends 30-50 years for transport infrastructure. Infrastructure Australia typically uses 50 years.

discount_rate.note: 7% central per OIA/ATAP. Victoria also reports at 4%. Sensitivity at 4% and 10%.

default_parameters.optimism_bias_approach: Reference Class Forecasting (P50/P90). Fixed percentage uplifts not used in Australian practice.
default_parameters.cost_estimate_basis: P90 recommended for budget submissions (VIC DTF guidance)

reference_bcr_range.note: Infrastructure Australia classifies: BCR < 1.0 at 7% = does not pass the threshold. BCR > 1.0 at 7% = passes. Note: a project with BCR 0.8 at 7% may have BCR > 1.0 at 4% (Victoria's lower rate).
reference_cases: Melbourne Metro Tunnel (2016 business case): BCR 1.5 at 7%, 2.3 at 4%
Victorian Level Crossing Removal (rolling programme): BCRs 1.2-4.0 per crossing (higher for high-frequency crossings)
Sydney Metro West (2020): BCR 1.1 at 7% (strategic connectivity case)
Inland Rail (Melbourne-Brisbane, 2015): BCR 1.02 at 7% (marginal, proceeded on national strategic grounds)

typical_risks: Cost overruns: Australian transport projects have comparable overrun rates to international benchmarks (Flyvbjerg data)
Demand forecasting: patronage forecasts for new public transport are particularly uncertain
Property acquisition (compulsory acquisition costs, delays, legal challenges)
Contaminated land (especially former industrial sites in inner urban areas)
Construction disruption to existing transport network
Political risk: long project timelines span state and federal election cycles

publications: ATAP Guidelines (Australian Transport Assessment and Planning)
